Works. Same output as the direct qemu-system invocation: harness verify loop top, kernel hidden.

Changes to `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`:
- `--system`: switches to `qemu-system-<arch>` and invokes with `-M -cpu -nographic -semihosting -bios <elf>` instead of `-- <elf>`.
- `--machine` (default `andes_ae350`), `--cpu` (default `andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024`), `--qemu-config` (forwards `-readconfig`), `--cwd` (chdir for semihosting fread relative paths), `--qemu-arg` (repeatable passthrough).
- User-mode path preserved.

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Hot inner-loop detector wrapper.
#
# Runs an ELF under user-mode QEMU with the hotloops plugin and prints
# a sorted table of detected backward branches:
#
#   iters | start_pc -> end_pc | function:offset
#
# Usage:
#   scripts/hotloops.sh [opts] <elf> [--] [prog args...]
#
# Options:
#   --arch <riscv32|riscv64|auto>   Target arch (default: auto)
#   --qemu <path>                   Override qemu binary
#   --plugin <path>                 Override plugin .so
#   --limit <N>                     Top-N loops to print (default 50)
#   --min-iters <N>                 Hot-loop threshold; drop loops with
#                                   fewer than N iterations (default 1)
#   --objdump <path>                Override objdump (resolves symbols)
#   --addr2line <path>              Override addr2line (resolves source lines)
#   --no-source                     Disable source file:line resolution
#   --raw                           Print raw plugin output, no formatting
#   --system                        Run baremetal ELF under system-mode QEMU
#                                   (qemu-system-<arch> -bios <elf>) instead
#                                   of user-mode emulation. Required for
#                                   Andes AE350 baremetal images (libnn).
#   --machine <name>                System mode: -M <name> (default andes_ae350)
#   --cpu <spec>                    System mode: -cpu <spec>
#                                   (default andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024)
#   --qemu-config <file>            System mode: -readconfig <file>
#   --cwd <dir>                     System mode: chdir before launching QEMU
#                                   so semihosting opens resolve relatively
#   --qemu-arg <arg>                Append an extra arg to the QEMU command
#                                   (repeatable; for things like extra -device)
#   -h | --help                     Show help

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd -- "$(dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
QEMU_ROOT="$(cd -- "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

ARCH="auto"
QEMU=""
PLUGIN=""
LIMIT=50
MIN_ITERS=1
OBJDUMP=""
ADDR2LINE=""
NO_SOURCE=0
RAW=0
SYSTEM_MODE=0
MACHINE="andes_ae350"
CPU="andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
QEMU_CONFIG=""
QEMU_CWD=""
EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS=()

usage() { sed -n '2,20p' "$0"; exit 0; }

# --- arg parsing ---
ELF=""
PROG_ARGS=()
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --arch)    ARCH="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu)    QEMU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --plugin)  PLUGIN="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --limit)   LIMIT="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --min-iters) MIN_ITERS="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --objdump) OBJDUMP="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --addr2line) ADDR2LINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --no-source) NO_SOURCE=1; shift ;;
        --raw)     RAW=1; shift ;;
        --system)  SYSTEM_MODE=1; shift ;;
        --machine) MACHINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --cpu)     CPU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu-config) QEMU_CONFIG="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --cwd)     QEMU_CWD="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu-arg) EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS+=("$2"); shift 2 ;;
        -h|--help) usage ;;
        --) shift; PROG_ARGS+=("$@"); break ;;
        -*) echo "unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 2 ;;
        *)
            if [[ -z "$ELF" ]]; then ELF="$1"; else PROG_ARGS+=("$1"); fi
            shift ;;
    esac
done

[[ -n "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: ELF path required" >&2; exit 2; }
[[ -f "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: not found: $ELF" >&2; exit 2; }

# --- arch detection ---
if [[ "$ARCH" == "auto" ]]; then
    file_out="$(file -L -- "$ELF" 2>/dev/null || true)"
    case "$file_out" in
        *"ELF 64-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv64 ;;
        *"ELF 32-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv32 ;;
        *) echo "error: cannot auto-detect arch (not RISC-V?), pass --arch" >&2; exit 2 ;;
    esac
fi

# --- locate qemu + plugin ---
if [[ -z "$QEMU" ]]; then
    if [[ "$SYSTEM_MODE" -eq 1 ]]; then
        QEMU="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/qemu-system-${ARCH}"
    else
        QEMU="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/qemu-${ARCH}"
    fi
fi
[[ -x "$QEMU" ]] || { echo "error: qemu not found/executable: $QEMU" >&2; exit 2; }

if [[ -z "$PLUGIN" ]]; then
    PLUGIN="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/contrib/plugins/libhotloops.so"
fi
[[ -f "$PLUGIN" ]] || { echo "error: plugin not built: $PLUGIN" >&2; exit 2; }

# --- run ---
LOG="$(mktemp -t hotloops.XXXXXX.log)"
trap 'rm -f "$LOG"' EXIT

if [[ "$SYSTEM_MODE" -eq 1 ]]; then
    [[ ${#PROG_ARGS[@]} -eq 0 ]] || {
        echo "error: --system mode does not accept program args (baremetal -bios)" >&2
        exit 2
    }
    QEMU_CMD=("$QEMU"
              -plugin "${PLUGIN},limit=${LIMIT},min_iters=${MIN_ITERS}"
              -d plugin -D "$LOG"
              -M "$MACHINE"
              -cpu "$CPU"
              -nographic -semihosting
              -bios "$ELF")
    [[ -n "$QEMU_CONFIG" ]] && QEMU_CMD+=(-readconfig "$QEMU_CONFIG")
    [[ ${#EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]] && QEMU_CMD+=("${EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]}")
    if [[ -n "$QEMU_CWD" ]]; then
        ( cd "$QEMU_CWD" && "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" ) >/dev/null
    else
        "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" >/dev/null
    fi
else
    USER_QEMU_CMD=("$QEMU"
                   -plugin "${PLUGIN},limit=${LIMIT},min_iters=${MIN_ITERS}"
                   -d plugin -D "$LOG")
    [[ ${#EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]] && USER_QEMU_CMD+=("${EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]}")
    USER_QEMU_CMD+=(-- "$ELF")
    [[ ${#PROG_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]] && USER_QEMU_CMD+=("${PROG_ARGS[@]}")
    "${USER_QEMU_CMD[@]}" >/dev/null
fi

if [[ "$RAW" -eq 1 ]]; then
    cat "$LOG"
    exit 0
fi

# --- symbol table from objdump (best effort) ---
if [[ -z "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
    for cand in llvm-objdump riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump \
                riscv32-unknown-elf-objdump objdump; do
        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            OBJDUMP="$cand"
            break
        fi
    done
fi

SYMS="$(mktemp -t hotloops_syms.XXXXXX)"
LINES="$(mktemp -t hotloops_lines.XXXXXX)"
trap 'rm -f "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES"' EXIT
if [[ -n "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
    # Capture FUNC symbols: addr size type name
    "$OBJDUMP" -t "$ELF" 2>/dev/null \
        | awk 'tolower($0) ~ /[ \t]f[ \t]/ && NF>=5 {
                  addr=strtonum("0x" $1);
                  size=strtonum("0x" $(NF-1));
                  name=$NF;
                  if (size>0) print addr, size, name;
              }' \
        | sort -n -k1,1 > "$SYMS" || true
fi

# --- locate addr2line for source line resolution (DWARF) ---
if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -z "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
    for cand in llvm-addr2line addr2line \
                riscv64-unknown-elf-addr2line riscv32-unknown-elf-addr2line \
                riscv64-linux-gnu-addr2line; do
        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            ADDR2LINE="$cand"
            break
        fi
    done
fi

# Pre-collect all PCs from the log and batch-resolve to file:line.
# LINES file format: <hex-pc> <file:line>
if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -n "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
    pcs="$(awk -F',' '/^0x/ {gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$1); gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$2); print $1; print $2}' "$LOG" | sort -u)"
    if [[ -n "$pcs" ]]; then
        # addr2line: -e <elf> -f -s -C reads addrs on stdin, prints
        # alternating function/file:line; we ask for file:line only via -p.
        out="$(printf '%s\n' $pcs | "$ADDR2LINE" -e "$ELF" -s 2>/dev/null || true)"
        paste <(printf '%s\n' $pcs) <(printf '%s\n' "$out") > "$LINES" || true
    fi
fi

# --- format ---
python3 - "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES" "$LIMIT" "$MIN_ITERS" <<'PY'
import sys, re

log_path = sys.argv[1]
syms_path = sys.argv[2]
lines_path = sys.argv[3]
limit = int(sys.argv[4])
min_iters = int(sys.argv[5])

syms = []
try:
    with open(syms_path) as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.split()
            if len(parts) >= 3:
                addr = int(parts[0])
                size = int(parts[1])
                name = " ".join(parts[2:])
                syms.append((addr, size, name))
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

def resolve(pc):
    for addr, size, name in syms:
        if addr <= pc < addr + size:
            return f"{name}+0x{pc-addr:x}"
    return "?"

src_map = {}
try:
    with open(lines_path) as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.rstrip("\n").split("\t", 1)
            if len(parts) == 2:
                pc_str, src = parts
                src = src.strip()
                if src and not src.startswith("??"):
                    try:
                        src_map[int(pc_str, 16)] = src
                    except ValueError:
                        pass
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

def src_line(pc):
    return src_map.get(pc, "")

rows = []
with open(log_path) as f:
    for line in f:
        m = re.match(r"\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(\d+)", line)
        if m:
            rows.append((int(m.group(1), 16),
                         int(m.group(2), 16),
                         int(m.group(3))))

if not rows:
    sys.stderr.write("hotloops: no backward edges observed\n")
    sys.exit(0)

rows = [r for r in rows if r[2] >= min_iters]
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r[2])
rows = rows[:limit]

if not rows:
    sys.stderr.write(f"hotloops: no loops with iters >= {min_iters}\n")
    sys.exit(0)

print(f"# hot loops (iters >= {min_iters}), sorted desc, top {len(rows)}")

have_src = bool(src_map)

w_iter = max(len("iters"), max(len(str(r[2])) for r in rows))
w_pc = 18  # 0x + 16 hex
w_sym = max(len("location"),
            max(len(f"{resolve(r[0])} -> {resolve(r[1])}") for r in rows))

if have_src:
    def srcpair(s, e):
        a, b = src_line(s), src_line(e)
        if a and b and a == b:
            return a
        return f"{a or '?'} -> {b or '?'}"
    w_src = max(len("source"),
                max(len(srcpair(r[0], r[1])) for r in rows))
    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | "
          f"{'location':<{w_sym}} | source")
    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym + 3 + w_src))
    for s, e, c in rows:
        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | "
              f"{loc:<{w_sym}} | {srcpair(s, e)}")
else:
    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | location")
    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym))
    for s, e, c in rows:
        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | {loc}")
PY

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Hot inner-loop detector wrapper (system-mode, baremetal RISC-V).
#
# Runs a baremetal ELF under qemu-system-<arch> with the hotloops plugin
# and prints a sorted table of detected backward branches:
#
#   iters | start_pc -> end_pc | function:offset
#
# Usage:
#   scripts/hotloops.sh [opts] <elf>
#
# Options:
#   --arch <riscv32|riscv64|auto>   Target arch (default: auto)
#   --qemu <path>                   Override qemu-system binary
#   --plugin <path>                 Override plugin .so
#   --machine <name>                -M <name> (default andes_ae350)
#   --cpu <spec>                    -cpu <spec>
#                                   (default andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024)
#   --qemu-config <file>            -readconfig <file> (e.g. AE350 device cfg)
#   --cwd <dir>                     chdir before launching QEMU so semihosting
#                                   opens (fread of bin/*.bin) resolve
#   --qemu-arg <arg>                Append an extra arg to the QEMU command
#                                   (repeatable)
#   --limit <N>                     Top-N loops to print (default 50)
#   --min-iters <N>                 Drop loops with fewer than N iters (default 1)
#   --objdump <path>                Override objdump (resolves symbols)
#   --addr2line <path>              Override addr2line (resolves source lines)
#   --no-source                     Disable source file:line resolution
#   --raw                           Print raw plugin output, no formatting
#   -h | --help                     Show help

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd -- "$(dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
QEMU_ROOT="$(cd -- "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

ARCH="auto"
QEMU=""
PLUGIN=""
LIMIT=50
MIN_ITERS=1
OBJDUMP=""
ADDR2LINE=""
NO_SOURCE=0
RAW=0
MACHINE="andes_ae350"
CPU="andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
QEMU_CONFIG=""
QEMU_CWD=""
EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS=()

usage() { sed -n '2,30p' "$0"; exit 0; }

# --- arg parsing ---
ELF=""
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --arch)        ARCH="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu)        QEMU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --plugin)      PLUGIN="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --machine)     MACHINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --cpu)         CPU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu-config) QEMU_CONFIG="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --cwd)         QEMU_CWD="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu-arg)    EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS+=("$2"); shift 2 ;;
        --limit)       LIMIT="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --min-iters)   MIN_ITERS="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --objdump)     OBJDUMP="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --addr2line)   ADDR2LINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --no-source)   NO_SOURCE=1; shift ;;
        --raw)         RAW=1; shift ;;
        -h|--help)     usage ;;
        -*) echo "unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 2 ;;
        *)
            if [[ -z "$ELF" ]]; then
                ELF="$1"; shift
            else
                echo "error: unexpected positional arg: $1" >&2; exit 2
            fi ;;
    esac
done

[[ -n "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: ELF path required" >&2; exit 2; }
[[ -f "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: not found: $ELF" >&2; exit 2; }

# --- arch detection ---
if [[ "$ARCH" == "auto" ]]; then
    file_out="$(file -L -- "$ELF" 2>/dev/null || true)"
    case "$file_out" in
        *"ELF 64-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv64 ;;
        *"ELF 32-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv32 ;;
        *) echo "error: cannot auto-detect arch (not RISC-V?), pass --arch" >&2; exit 2 ;;
    esac
fi

# --- locate qemu + plugin ---
if [[ -z "$QEMU" ]]; then
    QEMU="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/qemu-system-${ARCH}"
fi
[[ -x "$QEMU" ]] || { echo "error: qemu not found/executable: $QEMU" >&2; exit 2; }

if [[ -z "$PLUGIN" ]]; then
    PLUGIN="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/contrib/plugins/libhotloops.so"
fi
[[ -f "$PLUGIN" ]] || { echo "error: plugin not built: $PLUGIN" >&2; exit 2; }

# --- run ---
LOG="$(mktemp -t hotloops.XXXXXX.log)"
trap 'rm -f "$LOG"' EXIT

QEMU_CMD=("$QEMU"
          -plugin "${PLUGIN},limit=${LIMIT},min_iters=${MIN_ITERS}"
          -d plugin -D "$LOG"
          -M "$MACHINE"
          -cpu "$CPU"
          -nographic -semihosting
          -bios "$ELF")
[[ -n "$QEMU_CONFIG" ]] && QEMU_CMD+=(-readconfig "$QEMU_CONFIG")
[[ ${#EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]] && QEMU_CMD+=("${EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]}")
if [[ -n "$QEMU_CWD" ]]; then
    ( cd "$QEMU_CWD" && "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" ) >/dev/null
else
    "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" >/dev/null
fi

if [[ "$RAW" -eq 1 ]]; then
    cat "$LOG"
    exit 0
fi

# --- symbol table from objdump (best effort) ---
if [[ -z "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
    for cand in llvm-objdump riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump \
                riscv32-unknown-elf-objdump objdump; do
        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            OBJDUMP="$cand"
            break
        fi
    done
fi

SYMS="$(mktemp -t hotloops_syms.XXXXXX)"
LINES="$(mktemp -t hotloops_lines.XXXXXX)"
trap 'rm -f "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES"' EXIT
if [[ -n "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
    # Capture FUNC symbols: addr size type name
    "$OBJDUMP" -t "$ELF" 2>/dev/null \
        | awk 'tolower($0) ~ /[ \t]f[ \t]/ && NF>=5 {
                  addr=strtonum("0x" $1);
                  size=strtonum("0x" $(NF-1));
                  name=$NF;
                  if (size>0) print addr, size, name;
              }' \
        | sort -n -k1,1 > "$SYMS" || true
fi

# --- locate addr2line for source line resolution (DWARF) ---
if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -z "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
    for cand in llvm-addr2line addr2line \
                riscv64-unknown-elf-addr2line riscv32-unknown-elf-addr2line \
                riscv64-linux-gnu-addr2line; do
        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            ADDR2LINE="$cand"
            break
        fi
    done
fi

# Pre-collect all PCs from the log and batch-resolve to file:line.
# LINES file format: <hex-pc> <file:line>
if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -n "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
    pcs="$(awk -F',' '/^0x/ {gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$1); gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$2); print $1; print $2}' "$LOG" | sort -u)"
    if [[ -n "$pcs" ]]; then
        # addr2line: -e <elf> -f -s -C reads addrs on stdin, prints
        # alternating function/file:line; we ask for file:line only via -p.
        out="$(printf '%s\n' $pcs | "$ADDR2LINE" -e "$ELF" -s 2>/dev/null || true)"
        paste <(printf '%s\n' $pcs) <(printf '%s\n' "$out") > "$LINES" || true
    fi
fi

# --- format ---
python3 - "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES" "$LIMIT" "$MIN_ITERS" <<'PY'
import sys, re

log_path = sys.argv[1]
syms_path = sys.argv[2]
lines_path = sys.argv[3]
limit = int(sys.argv[4])
min_iters = int(sys.argv[5])

syms = []
try:
    with open(syms_path) as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.split()
            if len(parts) >= 3:
                addr = int(parts[0])
                size = int(parts[1])
                name = " ".join(parts[2:])
                syms.append((addr, size, name))
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

def resolve(pc):
    for addr, size, name in syms:
        if addr <= pc < addr + size:
            return f"{name}+0x{pc-addr:x}"
    return "?"

src_map = {}
try:
    with open(lines_path) as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.rstrip("\n").split("\t", 1)
            if len(parts) == 2:
                pc_str, src = parts
                src = src.strip()
                if src and not src.startswith("??"):
                    try:
                        src_map[int(pc_str, 16)] = src
                    except ValueError:
                        pass
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

def src_line(pc):
    return src_map.get(pc, "")

rows = []
with open(log_path) as f:
    for line in f:
        m = re.match(r"\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(\d+)", line)
        if m:
            rows.append((int(m.group(1), 16),
                         int(m.group(2), 16),
                         int(m.group(3))))

if not rows:
    sys.stderr.write("hotloops: no backward edges observed\n")
    sys.exit(0)

rows = [r for r in rows if r[2] >= min_iters]
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r[2])
rows = rows[:limit]

if not rows:
    sys.stderr.write(f"hotloops: no loops with iters >= {min_iters}\n")
    sys.exit(0)

print(f"# hot loops (iters >= {min_iters}), sorted desc, top {len(rows)}")

have_src = bool(src_map)

w_iter = max(len("iters"), max(len(str(r[2])) for r in rows))
w_pc = 18  # 0x + 16 hex
w_sym = max(len("location"),
            max(len(f"{resolve(r[0])} -> {resolve(r[1])}") for r in rows))

if have_src:
    def srcpair(s, e):
        a, b = src_line(s), src_line(e)
        if a and b and a == b:
            return a
        return f"{a or '?'} -> {b or '?'}"
    w_src = max(len("source"),
                max(len(srcpair(r[0], r[1])) for r in rows))
    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | "
          f"{'location':<{w_sym}} | source")
    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym + 3 + w_src))
    for s, e, c in rows:
        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | "
              f"{loc:<{w_sym}} | {srcpair(s, e)}")
else:
    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | location")
    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym))
    for s, e, c in rows:
        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | {loc}")
PY

content.content:
42	
43	There's a wrapper at `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`. It assumes you have a *Linux* binary that runs under user-mode QEMU (`qemu-riscv64`). Our libnn perf ELFs are *baremetal* - they need system-mode QEMU with `-M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -bios <elf>`. The wrapper can't do that. So if `/optimize` integrates A, it must call `qemu-system-riscv64` directly, not via the wrapper.
44	
45	### Problem 2 - top-1 is not always the kernel
46	
47	For the *light* kernel `relu_s8`, the inner loop ran only 26 times. But the test harness has an accuracy-check loop that compares 65,534 output bytes one-by-one - so *that* loop topped the list with 65,534 iters, drowning out the 26-iter kernel:
48	
49	```

file.content:
There's a wrapper at `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`. It assumes you have a *Linux* binary that runs under user-mode QEMU (`qemu-riscv64`). Our libnn perf ELFs are *baremetal* - they need system-mode QEMU with `-M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -bios <elf>`. The wrapper can't do that. So if `/optimize` integrates A, it must call `qemu-system-riscv64` directly, not via the wrapper.

### Problem 2 - top-1 is not always the kernel

For the *light* kernel `relu_s8`, the inner loop ran only 26 times. But the test harness has an accuracy-check loop that compares 65,534 output bytes one-by-one - so *that* loop topped the list with 65,534 iters, drowning out the 26-iter kernel:

```
65534 | test+0x104 -> +0x128       <- harness verify loop, NOT the kernel
 1664 | memcpy+0xac -> +0xfe
  170 | memset+0x18 -> +0x1e
  ...
   26 | riscv_nn_relu_s8+0x58 -> +0xa6   <- the actual kernel, way down
```

If `/optimize` blindly takes rank-1, it optimizes the wrong loop. Fix is easy: read the ELF symbol table, drop any row whose PC isn't inside `riscv_nn_<your_function>` (or one of its callees like `nn_mat_mul_kernel_*`).

---

## Tool B: vsim Kanata loop-window

**This is where I hit real problems.**

### Note - top-1 is not always the kernel (by design)

For the *light* kernel `relu_s8`, the inner loop ran only 26 times. But the test harness has an accuracy-check loop that compares 65,534 output bytes one-by-one - so *that* loop topped the list with 65,534 iters, well above the 26-iter kernel:

```
65534 | test+0x104 -> +0x128       <- harness verify loop, NOT the kernel
 1664 | memcpy+0xac -> +0xfe
  170 | memset+0x18 -> +0x1e
  ...
   26 | riscv_nn_relu_s8+0x58 -> +0xa6   <- the actual kernel
```

This is *not* a bug. The table is a menu - the human or LLM driving `/optimize` reads it and picks which kernel loop to inspect. Symbol names (`riscv_nn_*`, `nn_mat_mul_kernel_*` vs `test+...`, `memcpy+...`) make the choice obvious. No auto-rank-1 logic needed; pushing that decision into the script would just hide context the picker wants to see.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# QEMU hotloops + vsim Kanata loop-window: verification on libnn

## What I tried to do

You asked me to verify two tools end-to-end on real libnn binaries, before wiring them into `/optimize`:

- **Tool A (QEMU hotloops)** - feed an ELF to QEMU, it tells you "the hottest loops are at these PC addresses".
- **Tool B (vsim Kanata loop-window)** - feed an ELF to vsim *plus* the PC range of one loop, vsim dumps a Konata pipeline trace for just that loop and exits.

The intended flow: A finds the loop, B dumps its pipeline. Together they replace the manual "extract a synthetic kernel into vsim" step.

I picked two existing libnn perf ELFs:
- `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast_perf.adx` (heavy - 524k FPGA cycles)
- `relu_s8_perf.adx` (light - 3k FPGA cycles)

Both already built without `-g`, as you required.

---

## Tool A: QEMU hotloops

**It works in principle.** I ran QEMU in system-mode with the plugin attached:

```
qemu-system-riscv64 -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=1024 \
  -nographic -semihosting -bios <elf> \
  -plugin libhotloops.so -d plugin -D log.txt
```

Output for the conv ELF:

```
12800 | 0x11288 -> 0x11308 | nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft_unroll4+0x88 -> +0x108
 1600 | 0x10be8 -> 0x10c20 | riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast+0x2d0 ...
 1416 | 0x133ec -> 0x1343e | memcpy+0xac -> memcpy+0xfe
 ...
```

The top loop is the actual GEMM inner body (8 `vle8.v` + 16 `nds.vd4dots` + `bnez`). PCs match the back-branch. So *for heavy kernels*, the tool gives the right answer.

### Problem 1 - the wrapper script is wrong for our use case

There's a wrapper at `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`. It assumes you have a *Linux* binary that runs under user-mode QEMU (`qemu-riscv64`). Our libnn perf ELFs are *baremetal* - they need system-mode QEMU with `-M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -bios <elf>`. The wrapper can't do that. So if `/optimize` integrates A, it must call `qemu-system-riscv64` directly, not via the wrapper.

### Problem 2 - top-1 is not always the kernel

For the *light* kernel `relu_s8`, the inner loop ran only 26 times. But the test harness has an accuracy-check loop that compares 65,534 output bytes one-by-one - so *that* loop topped the list with 65,534 iters, drowning out the 26-iter kernel:

```
65534 | test+0x104 -> +0x128       <- harness verify loop, NOT the kernel
 1664 | memcpy+0xac -> +0xfe
  170 | memset+0x18 -> +0x1e
  ...
   26 | riscv_nn_relu_s8+0x58 -> +0xa6   <- the actual kernel, way down
```

If `/optimize` blindly takes rank-1, it optimizes the wrong loop. Fix is easy: read the ELF symbol table, drop any row whose PC isn't inside `riscv_nn_<your_function>` (or one of its callees like `nn_mat_mul_kernel_*`).

---

## Tool B: vsim Kanata loop-window

**This is where I hit real problems.**

The doc promises: pass `--kanata-loop-start <pc> --kanata-loop-end <pc> --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4` and vsim will:
- Suppress all pipeline log output until the gate opens.
- Record exactly 4 iterations of the chosen loop.
- Print `Kanata loop window [...] : 4 iters, ... IPC=...` and exit cleanly.

I ran it on `relu_s8` with the PCs that hotloops gave me (`0x105f4 -> 0x10642`):

```
sim_ax45mpv_premium relu_s8_perf.adx \
  --kanata out.kanata \
  --kanata-loop-start 0x105f4 --kanata-loop-end 0x10642 \
  --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4
```

After **19 minutes of CPU time**, vsim was still running. I had to kill it at the 10-minute wallclock timeout. When I inspected what it had produced:

### Issue 1 - the gate does not filter

`out.kanata` was 28 MB containing 266,978 instruction records, with PC labels starting at `0x10140` (the very first instruction of CRT, long before our `--kanata-loop-start = 0x105f4`). The doc says: *"While the gate is closed, no I/C/R records are emitted."* In practice, vsim emitted everything from cycle 0. The gate is configured (vsim prints `[info] Kanata loop gate: start=... end=... skip=0 record=4` at startup) but it doesn't actually suppress output.

### Issue 2 - the record-complete exit never fires

Even if the gate had filtered, vsim is supposed to exit after the 4th iter. It didn't. No summary line was printed. vsim ran forever and had to be SIGKILLed.

### Issue 3 - vsim never even reached the kernel

This is the most damaging finding. I grepped the trace for any PC in the relu_s8 region (`0x1059c-0x10670`). **Zero hits.** vsim had executed 4 million simulated cycles' worth of code but had not yet entered `relu_s8`. It was stuck inside the libgloss/HTIF `fread` path that loads 65,534 bytes of golden-output data into memory before the kernel runs. vsim is roughly 8 kHz; reading 65k bytes one syscall at a time is a wall.

The implication is bigger than the gate bugs: even if Issues 1 and 2 are fixed, simply running a perf-test ELF inside vsim is not viable - the data-load prelude alone takes hours of wallclock. The reason `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` exists in the skills directory is precisely to dodge this: it builds a tiny synthetic harness with the kernel preloaded into a buffer, no fread, no golden data.

---

## Why this matters for `/optimize`

The clean-looking idea was:

```
hotloops -> (start_pc, end_pc) -> vsim Kanata gate on full perf ELF -> pipeline log
```

That can't work today. Two of the three steps each have showstopper issues for our real binaries.

The salvageable plan:

```
hotloops on full perf ELF (works, with two fixes)
  |
  v  start_pc, end_pc
  |
hot-inner-kernel-extractor builds a synthetic harness for that loop
  |
  v  small ELF that hits the kernel in milliseconds
  |
vsim Kanata gate on the synthetic ELF (gate bugs may matter less here)
  |
  v Konata pipeline trace
```

In other words: keep the existing extractor in the loop. Use hotloops *only* to pick which loop to feed it.

---

## What needs to be filed before integration

Against `hotloops.sh`: add a baremetal/system-mode path so it can launch `qemu-system-riscv64 -M ... -cpu ... -bios <elf>` instead of user-mode `qemu-riscv64 -- <elf>`.

Against vsim: the loop-window gate doesn't gate (Issue 1) and doesn't exit on `record` complete (Issue 2). Reproducer:

- ELF: `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/work/t_relu_s8_perf.adx`
- Flags: `--kanata-loop-start 0x105f4 --kanata-loop-end 0x10642 --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4`

Both need fixing before vsim Kanata can be a useful direct target.

So if `/optimize` integrates A, it must call `qemu-system-riscv64` directly, not via the wrapper.

content.text:
Shown. Confirms the picture:

- `size = 65535` (test-3 only, since `ENA_MEASURE_PERF` is defined)
- `test_Fread(in_out, 1, 65535, ...)` -> the 65k-byte semihosting fread that vsim chokes on
- `riscv_nn_relu_s8(in_out, 65535)` called once -> the 26-iter outer loop
- `test_Fread(golden, 1, 65535, ...)` -> another 65k semihosting read
- `verify_buffer(in_out, golden, 65535, 1, 0)` -> the 65534-iter byte compare loop that topped hotloops

So in vsim: two 65k fread calls + one verify loop + the kernel itself. The fread prelude alone is what kills wallclock before the kernel runs.

input.new_string:
### Note - vsim is slow, slim the harness before launching

I grepped the trace for any PC in the relu_s8 region (`0x1059c-0x10670`). **Zero hits.** vsim had executed 4 million simulated cycles' worth of code but had not yet entered `relu_s8`. It was stuck inside the libgloss/HTIF `fread` path that loads 65,534 bytes of input data into memory before the kernel runs. vsim is roughly 8 kHz; reading 65k bytes one syscall at a time is a wall.

This is not a vsim bug; it is a workflow expectation. vsim is too slow to swallow the full unit-test harness as-is. Before running a Kanata loop-window dump, the human/LLM driving `/optimize` must shrink everything between `_start` and the kernel call:

- Drop the input `test_Fread` (preload a small in-memory buffer, or a static array).
- Drop the golden `test_Fread`.
- Drop `verify_buffer` and any post-kernel accuracy check.
- Optionally cut the input size (e.g., `size = 65535` -> something just large enough that the inner loop reaches steady state, plus the warmup `--kanata-loop-skip` count).

Two ways to do this in practice:

- Edit `t_<fn>.c` directly into a stripped variant for vsim runs.
- Use `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` (already in `.claude/skills/`) which builds a synthetic harness with the kernel input preloaded statically - no fread, no golden, no verify.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# QEMU hotloops + vsim Kanata loop-window: verification on libnn

## What I tried to do

You asked me to verify two tools end-to-end on real libnn binaries, before wiring them into `/optimize`:

- **Tool A (QEMU hotloops)** - feed an ELF to QEMU, it tells you "the hottest loops are at these PC addresses".
- **Tool B (vsim Kanata loop-window)** - feed an ELF to vsim *plus* the PC range of one loop, vsim dumps a Konata pipeline trace for just that loop and exits.

The intended flow: A finds the loop, B dumps its pipeline. Together they replace the manual "extract a synthetic kernel into vsim" step.

I picked two existing libnn perf ELFs:
- `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast_perf.adx` (heavy - 524k FPGA cycles)
- `relu_s8_perf.adx` (light - 3k FPGA cycles)

Both already built without `-g`, as you required.

---

## Tool A: QEMU hotloops

**It works in principle.** I ran QEMU in system-mode with the plugin attached:

```
qemu-system-riscv64 -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=1024 \
  -nographic -semihosting -bios <elf> \
  -plugin libhotloops.so -d plugin -D log.txt
```

Output for the conv ELF:

```
12800 | 0x11288 -> 0x11308 | nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft_unroll4+0x88 -> +0x108
 1600 | 0x10be8 -> 0x10c20 | riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast+0x2d0 ...
 1416 | 0x133ec -> 0x1343e | memcpy+0xac -> memcpy+0xfe
 ...
```

The top loop is the actual GEMM inner body (8 `vle8.v` + 16 `nds.vd4dots` + `bnez`). PCs match the back-branch. So *for heavy kernels*, the tool gives the right answer.

### Problem 1 - the wrapper script is wrong for our use case

There's a wrapper at `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`. It assumes you have a *Linux* binary that runs under user-mode QEMU (`qemu-riscv64`). Our libnn perf ELFs are *baremetal* - they need system-mode QEMU with `-M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -bios <elf>`. The wrapper can't do that. So if `/optimize` integrates A, it must call `qemu-system-riscv64` directly, not via the wrapper.

### Note - top-1 is not always the kernel (by design)

For the *light* kernel `relu_s8`, the inner loop ran only 26 times. But the test harness has an accuracy-check loop that compares 65,534 output bytes one-by-one - so *that* loop topped the list with 65,534 iters, well above the 26-iter kernel:

```
65534 | test+0x104 -> +0x128       <- harness verify loop, NOT the kernel
 1664 | memcpy+0xac -> +0xfe
  170 | memset+0x18 -> +0x1e
  ...
   26 | riscv_nn_relu_s8+0x58 -> +0xa6   <- the actual kernel
```

This is *not* a bug. The table is a menu - the human or LLM driving `/optimize` reads it and picks which kernel loop to inspect. Symbol names (`riscv_nn_*`, `nn_mat_mul_kernel_*` vs `test+...`, `memcpy+...`) make the choice obvious. No auto-rank-1 logic needed; pushing that decision into the script would just hide context the picker wants to see.

---

## Tool B: vsim Kanata loop-window

**This is where I hit real problems.**

The doc promises: pass `--kanata-loop-start <pc> --kanata-loop-end <pc> --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4` and vsim will:
- Suppress all pipeline log output until the gate opens.
- Record exactly 4 iterations of the chosen loop.
- Print `Kanata loop window [...] : 4 iters, ... IPC=...` and exit cleanly.

I ran it on `relu_s8` with the PCs that hotloops gave me (`0x105f4 -> 0x10642`):

```
sim_ax45mpv_premium relu_s8_perf.adx \
  --kanata out.kanata \
  --kanata-loop-start 0x105f4 --kanata-loop-end 0x10642 \
  --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4
```

After **19 minutes of CPU time**, vsim was still running. I had to kill it at the 10-minute wallclock timeout. When I inspected what it had produced:

### Issue 1 - the gate does not filter

`out.kanata` was 28 MB containing 266,978 instruction records, with PC labels starting at `0x10140` (the very first instruction of CRT, long before our `--kanata-loop-start = 0x105f4`). The doc says: *"While the gate is closed, no I/C/R records are emitted."* In practice, vsim emitted everything from cycle 0. The gate is configured (vsim prints `[info] Kanata loop gate: start=... end=... skip=0 record=4` at startup) but it doesn't actually suppress output.

### Issue 2 - the record-complete exit never fires

Even if the gate had filtered, vsim is supposed to exit after the 4th iter. It didn't. No summary line was printed. vsim ran forever and had to be SIGKILLed.

### Issue 3 - vsim never even reached the kernel

This is the most damaging finding. I grepped the trace for any PC in the relu_s8 region (`0x1059c-0x10670`). **Zero hits.** vsim had executed 4 million simulated cycles' worth of code but had not yet entered `relu_s8`. It was stuck inside the libgloss/HTIF `fread` path that loads 65,534 bytes of golden-output data into memory before the kernel runs. vsim is roughly 8 kHz; reading 65k bytes one syscall at a time is a wall.

The implication is bigger than the gate bugs: even if Issues 1 and 2 are fixed, simply running a perf-test ELF inside vsim is not viable - the data-load prelude alone takes hours of wallclock. The reason `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` exists in the skills directory is precisely to dodge this: it builds a tiny synthetic harness with the kernel preloaded into a buffer, no fread, no golden data.

---

## Why this matters for `/optimize`

The clean-looking idea was:

```
hotloops -> (start_pc, end_pc) -> vsim Kanata gate on full perf ELF -> pipeline log
```

That can't work today. Two of the three steps each have showstopper issues for our real binaries.

The salvageable plan:

```
hotloops on full perf ELF (works, with two fixes)
  |
  v  start_pc, end_pc
  |
hot-inner-kernel-extractor builds a synthetic harness for that loop
  |
  v  small ELF that hits the kernel in milliseconds
  |
vsim Kanata gate on the synthetic ELF (gate bugs may matter less here)
  |
  v Konata pipeline trace
```

In other words: keep the existing extractor in the loop. Use hotloops *only* to pick which loop to feed it.

---

## What needs to be filed before integration

Against `hotloops.sh`: add a baremetal/system-mode path so it can launch `qemu-system-riscv64 -M ... -cpu ... -bios <elf>` instead of user-mode `qemu-riscv64 -- <elf>`.

Against vsim: the loop-window gate doesn't gate (Issue 1) and doesn't exit on `record` complete (Issue 2). Reproducer:

- ELF: `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/work/t_relu_s8_perf.adx`
- Flags: `--kanata-loop-start 0x105f4 --kanata-loop-end 0x10642 --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4`

Both need fixing before vsim Kanata can be a useful direct target.

lines: Even if the gate had filtered, vsim is supposed to exit after the 4th iter. It didn't. No summary line was printed. vsim ran forever and had to be SIGKILLed.
-### Issue 3 - vsim never even reached the kernel
+### Note - vsim is slow, slim the harness before launching

-This is the most damaging finding. I grepped the trace for any PC in the relu_s8 region (`0x1059c-0x10670`). **Zero hits.** vsim had executed 4 million simulated cycles' worth of code but had not yet entered `relu_s8`. It was stuck inside the libgloss/HTIF `fread` path that loads 65,534 bytes of golden-output data into memory before the kernel runs. vsim is roughly 8 kHz; reading 65k bytes one syscall at a time is a wall.

+I grepped the trace for any PC in the relu_s8 region (`0x1059c-0x10670`). **Zero hits.** vsim had executed 4 million simulated cycles' worth of code but had not yet entered `relu_s8`. It was stuck inside the libgloss/HTIF `fread` path that loads 65,534 bytes of input data into memory before the kernel runs. vsim is roughly 8 kHz; reading 65k bytes one syscall at a time is a wall.

-The implication is bigger than the gate bugs: even if Issues 1 and 2 are fixed, simply running a perf-test ELF inside vsim is not viable - the data-load prelude alone takes hours of wallclock. The reason `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` exists in the skills directory is precisely to dodge this: it builds a tiny synthetic harness with the kernel preloaded into a buffer, no fread, no golden data.

+This is not a vsim bug; it is a workflow expectation. vsim is too slow to swallow the full unit-test harness as-is. Before running a Kanata loop-window dump, the human/LLM driving `/optimize` must shrink everything between `_start` and the kernel call:
+- Drop the input `test_Fread` (preload a small in-memory buffer, or a static array).
+- Drop `verify_buffer` and any post-kernel accuracy check.
+- Optionally cut the input size (e.g., `size = 65535` -> something just large enough that the inner loop reaches steady state, plus the warmup `--kanata-loop-skip` count).

+- Edit `t_<fn>.c` directly into a stripped variant for vsim runs.
+- Use `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` (already in `.claude/skills/`) which builds a synthetic harness with the kernel input preloaded statically - no fread, no golden, no verify.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is iterating on `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/tmp.md` (a verification report for QEMU hotloops + vsim Kanata loop-window tools) and on `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh` (the QEMU plugin wrapper). Specific requests in chronological order:
   - "read tmp.md, tool A, issue 1, change the script to use system model qemu as libnn did" - Add system-mode support to hotloops.sh matching libnn's QEMU invocation pattern.
   - "remove user-mode completely" - Strip all user-mode logic from the wrapper.
   - "tool A, problem 2, That's designed for human or LLM to decide which kernel loop they wanna inspect." - Reframe the "top-1 not always kernel" finding as expected behavior, not a bug.
   - "tool B, problem 1, show me the code of relu" - Display the relu_s8 kernel source.
   - "I mean the main function before calling the relu" - Clarification: show the test harness, not the kernel.
   - "vsim is extremely slow, so one of the responsibility for human or LLM is to reduce the code before calling the kernel as much as possible. like disabling the accuracy check and the verification" - Reframe vsim's slowness (Issue 3) as a workflow expectation requiring harness slimming, not a vsim bug.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - QEMU TCG plugin (libhotloops.so) for backward-edge loop detection
   - User-mode QEMU (qemu-riscv64) vs system-mode QEMU (qemu-system-riscv64)
   - Andes baremetal AE350 machine (`andes_ae350` machine, `andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024` CPU)
   - libnn perf test harness pattern (test_Fread + kernel + verify_buffer)
   - vsim Kanata pipeline dump and loop-window gate (skip/record)
   - HTIF/libgloss fread bottleneck (vsim ~8 kHz cannot tolerate 65k-byte fread)
   - hot-inner-kernel-extractor synthetic harness skill (preloaded buffer, no fread/verify)
   - Symbol-table-based menu for human/LLM kernel selection
   - RVV 1.0 + Andes vendor extensions (vmax, vle8.v, etc.)

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`
     - The QEMU plugin wrapper. Originally hardcoded user-mode `qemu-${arch}` and `-- ELF` argv style, unusable for libnn baremetal images.
     - Completely rewritten to system-mode only after user requested user-mode removal.
     - New options: `--machine` (default `andes_ae350`), `--cpu` (default `andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024`), `--qemu-config`, `--cwd`, `--qemu-arg`.
     - Key invocation block:
       ```bash
       QEMU_CMD=("$QEMU"
                 -plugin "${PLUGIN},limit=${LIMIT},min_iters=${MIN_ITERS}"
                 -d plugin -D "$LOG"
                 -M "$MACHINE"
                 -cpu "$CPU"
                 -nographic -semihosting
                 -bios "$ELF")
       [[ -n "$QEMU_CONFIG" ]] && QEMU_CMD+=(-readconfig "$QEMU_CONFIG")
       [[ ${#EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]] && QEMU_CMD+=("${EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]}")
       if [[ -n "$QEMU_CWD" ]]; then
           ( cd "$QEMU_CWD" && "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" ) >/dev/null
       else
           "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" >/dev/null
       fi
       ```
     - Default qemu binary path is now `${QEMU_ROOT}/build/qemu-system-${ARCH}`.

   - `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/tmp.md`
     - The verification report. Multiple edits to reframe findings:
       - "Problem 2 - top-1 is not always the kernel" -> "Note - top-1 is not always the kernel (by design)". Explains the ranked table is a menu; symbol names (`riscv_nn_*` vs `test+...`, `memcpy+...`) make the choice obvious; no auto-rank-1 logic warranted.
       - "Issue 3 - vsim never even reached the kernel" -> "Note - vsim is slow, slim the harness before launching". Lists actions: drop input test_Fread, drop golden test_Fread, drop verify_buffer, optionally cut input size. Two slim-down paths: hand-edit `t_<fn>.c`, or use `hot-inner-kernel-extractor`.
       - "Why this matters" plan diagram updated to remove "showstopper" framing and show working plan: hotloops -> menu-pick -> slim harness -> vsim Kanata gate (Issues 1 and 2 still need fixing).

   - `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/script/run_qemu_test.sh` (read-only)
     - Source of the libnn QEMU invocation pattern. Showed the exact command:
       ```
       QEMU_CMD=(${CFG_QEMU_BIN} -readconfig ${CFG_QEMU_CONFIG}
                 -M ${CFG_QEMU_MACHINE}
                 -cpu ${CFG_QEMU_CPU}
                 -nographic -semihosting -bios ${ADX})
       ```
     - Also showed the perf test runs from `Examples/unit_func/` so semihosting fread of `bin/8b_in1.bin` resolves.

   - `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/Source/ActivationFunctions/riscv_nn_relu_s8.c` (read-only)
     - Multiple ENA paths (ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC, ENA_VEC_ISA, ENA_DSP_ISA, plain C). For AX45MPV the ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC path is taken: 4x unrolled main loop processing `maxvl_per_loop = VLMAX*4` bytes per iter, plus a tail VL loop. The hotloops `riscv_nn_relu_s8+0x58 -> +0xa6` (26 iters) is the main 4x unrolled loop.

   - `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/Examples/unit_func/t_relu_s8.c` (read-only)
     - The test harness. Confirmed structure:
       - `size = 65535` for perf test (`ENA_MEASURE_PERF` defined, only test-3 runs)
       - `test_Fread(in_out, 1, 65535, ...)` - 65k semihosting fread (vsim wall)
       - `riscv_nn_relu_s8(in_out, 65535)` - kernel call (~26 outer iters)
       - `test_Fread(golden, 1, 65535, ...)` - second 65k fread
       - `verify_buffer(in_out, golden, 65535, 1, 0)` - 65534-iter byte compare (the loop topping hotloops)

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Wrapper fails on baremetal ELF with "Illegal instruction"**: Confirmed by running original `hotloops.sh` on `t_relu_s8_perf.adx`. Fixed by adding `--system` mode to invoke `qemu-system-${arch}` with full machine/cpu/-bios path.
   - **Empty array expansion under `set -u`**: Initial `--system` integration risked `${EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]}` empty-expansion issues. Guarded with `[[ ${#EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]]` checks before append.
   - **User feedback on user-mode**: User explicitly said "remove user-mode completely" after I had preserved both modes. Responded by full Write rewrite removing user-mode entirely.
   - **User feedback on Problem 2**: User clarified the ranked table is intentionally a menu for human/LLM picking. Responded by reframing the section as "Note" rather than "Problem".
   - **User feedback on Issue 3**: User clarified vsim slowness is a known workflow constraint requiring harness slimming, not a vsim bug. Responded by reframing as "Note" and adding two slim-down paths.
   - **User feedback "show me the code of relu" -> "I mean the main function"**: I initially showed the kernel source; user clarified they meant the test harness. Showed `t_relu_s8.c` instead.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Resolved**: hotloops.sh now supports baremetal Andes ELFs via system-mode QEMU. Verified output matches direct `qemu-system-riscv64` invocation.
   - **Resolved**: tmp.md narrative now correctly distinguishes real vsim bugs (Issues 1 and 2: gate doesn't filter, doesn't exit on record-complete) from workflow expectations (top-1 menu picking, harness slimming for vsim speed).
   - **Confirmed**: relu_s8 test harness has two 65k freads + 65k verify loop around a single kernel call - confirms why vsim cannot reach the kernel without slimming.

6. All user messages:
   - "read tmp.md, tool A, issue 1, change the script to use system model qemu as libnn did"
   - "remove user-mode completely"
   - "tool A, problem 2, That's designed for human or LLM to decide which kernel loop they wanna inspect."
   - "tool B, problem 1, show me the code of relu"
   - "I mean the main function before calling the relu"
   - "vsim is extremely slow, so one of the repsonsibility for human or LLM is to reduce the code before calling the kernel as much as possible. like disabling the accuracy check and the verification"

7. Pending Tasks:
   None explicitly stated. All requested edits to hotloops.sh and tmp.md are complete.

8. Current Work:
   The most recent work was updating `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/tmp.md` to reframe Issue 3 (vsim never reached the kernel) and the "Why this matters for /optimize" plan diagram. Two edits were made:
   - Issue 3 -> "Note - vsim is slow, slim the harness before launching" with action list (drop input fread, drop golden fread, drop verify_buffer, optionally cut input size) and two implementation paths (hand-edit `t_<fn>.c` or use `hot-inner-kernel-extractor`).
   - "Why this matters" plan diagram updated to remove "showstopper" framing:
     ```
     hotloops on full perf ELF (works)
       |
       v  start_pc, end_pc, picked by human/LLM from the symbol-named menu
       |
     slim the harness:
       - hot-inner-kernel-extractor (synthetic harness, kernel input static), OR
       - edit t_<fn>.c to drop fread / golden / verify_buffer
       |
       v  small ELF that hits the kernel in milliseconds
       |
     vsim Kanata gate on the slim ELF (still need Issues 1 and 2 fixed)
       |
       v Konata pipeline trace
     ```
   I confirmed completion with: "Updated. Issue 3 is now framed as a workflow expectation (slim the harness before vsim), not a vsim bug. The plan diagram shows two slim-down options: `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` or hand-editing `t_<fn>.c` to drop fread/golden/verify."

9. Optional Next Step:
   No explicit next step from the user. The user has been walking through tmp.md section by section ("tool A, problem 2", "tool B, problem 1", then Issue 3 reframing). The two remaining tmp.md sections that have not been revisited are:
   - Tool B Issue 2 (record-complete exit never fires) - still framed as a vsim bug.
   - "What needs to be filed before integration" section listing both bug reports.
   
   The user may continue this section-by-section walkthrough (e.g., next addressing Issue 2), but I should wait for explicit direction rather than preemptively editing other sections, since the pattern has been one user message per section and the user controls the framing.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/nick/.claude/projects/-home-nick-work-libnn-0421/fb42e6cb-c844-4380-94c3-c512f6da092b.jsonl

file.content:
# QEMU hotloops + vsim Kanata loop-window: verification on libnn

## What I tried to do

You asked me to verify two tools end-to-end on real libnn binaries, before wiring them into `/optimize`:

- **Tool A (QEMU hotloops)** - feed an ELF to QEMU, it tells you "the hottest loops are at these PC addresses".
- **Tool B (vsim Kanata loop-window)** - feed an ELF to vsim *plus* the PC range of one loop, vsim dumps a Konata pipeline trace for just that loop and exits.

The intended flow: A finds the loop, B dumps its pipeline. Together they replace the manual "extract a synthetic kernel into vsim" step.

I picked two existing libnn perf ELFs:
- `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast_perf.adx` (heavy - 524k FPGA cycles)
- `relu_s8_perf.adx` (light - 3k FPGA cycles)

Both already built without `-g`, as you required.

---

## Tool A: QEMU hotloops

**It works in principle.** I ran QEMU in system-mode with the plugin attached:

```
qemu-system-riscv64 -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=1024 \
  -nographic -semihosting -bios <elf> \
  -plugin libhotloops.so -d plugin -D log.txt
```

Output for the conv ELF:

```
12800 | 0x11288 -> 0x11308 | nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft_unroll4+0x88 -> +0x108
 1600 | 0x10be8 -> 0x10c20 | riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast+0x2d0 ...
 1416 | 0x133ec -> 0x1343e | memcpy+0xac -> memcpy+0xfe
 ...
```

The top loop is the actual GEMM inner body (8 `vle8.v` + 16 `nds.vd4dots` + `bnez`). PCs match the back-branch. So *for heavy kernels*, the tool gives the right answer.

### Problem 1 - the wrapper script is wrong for our use case

There's a wrapper at `/local/nick/qemu_v5/scripts/hotloops.sh`. It assumes you have a *Linux* binary that runs under user-mode QEMU (`qemu-riscv64`). Our libnn perf ELFs are *baremetal* - they need system-mode QEMU with `-M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -bios <elf>`. The wrapper can't do that. So if `/optimize` integrates A, it must call `qemu-system-riscv64` directly, not via the wrapper.

### Note - top-1 is not always the kernel (by design)

For the *light* kernel `relu_s8`, the inner loop ran only 26 times. But the test harness has an accuracy-check loop that compares 65,534 output bytes one-by-one - so *that* loop topped the list with 65,534 iters, well above the 26-iter kernel:

```
65534 | test+0x104 -> +0x128       <- harness verify loop, NOT the kernel
 1664 | memcpy+0xac -> +0xfe
  170 | memset+0x18 -> +0x1e
  ...
   26 | riscv_nn_relu_s8+0x58 -> +0xa6   <- the actual kernel
```

This is *not* a bug. The table is a menu - the human or LLM driving `/optimize` reads it and picks which kernel loop to inspect. Symbol names (`riscv_nn_*`, `nn_mat_mul_kernel_*` vs `test+...`, `memcpy+...`) make the choice obvious. No auto-rank-1 logic needed; pushing that decision into the script would just hide context the picker wants to see.

---

## Tool B: vsim Kanata loop-window

**This is where I hit real problems.**

The doc promises: pass `--kanata-loop-start <pc> --kanata-loop-end <pc> --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4` and vsim will:
- Suppress all pipeline log output until the gate opens.
- Record exactly 4 iterations of the chosen loop.
- Print `Kanata loop window [...] : 4 iters, ... IPC=...` and exit cleanly.

I ran it on `relu_s8` with the PCs that hotloops gave me (`0x105f4 -> 0x10642`):

```
sim_ax45mpv_premium relu_s8_perf.adx \
  --kanata out.kanata \
  --kanata-loop-start 0x105f4 --kanata-loop-end 0x10642 \
  --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4
```

After **19 minutes of CPU time**, vsim was still running. I had to kill it at the 10-minute wallclock timeout. When I inspected what it had produced:

### Issue 1 - the gate does not filter

`out.kanata` was 28 MB containing 266,978 instruction records, with PC labels starting at `0x10140` (the very first instruction of CRT, long before our `--kanata-loop-start = 0x105f4`). The doc says: *"While the gate is closed, no I/C/R records are emitted."* In practice, vsim emitted everything from cycle 0. The gate is configured (vsim prints `[info] Kanata loop gate: start=... end=... skip=0 record=4` at startup) but it doesn't actually suppress output.

### Issue 2 - the record-complete exit never fires

Even if the gate had filtered, vsim is supposed to exit after the 4th iter. It didn't. No summary line was printed. vsim ran forever and had to be SIGKILLed.

### Note - vsim is slow, slim the harness before launching

I grepped the trace for any PC in the relu_s8 region (`0x1059c-0x10670`). **Zero hits.** vsim had executed 4 million simulated cycles' worth of code but had not yet entered `relu_s8`. It was stuck inside the libgloss/HTIF `fread` path that loads 65,534 bytes of input data into memory before the kernel runs. vsim is roughly 8 kHz; reading 65k bytes one syscall at a time is a wall.

This is not a vsim bug; it is a workflow expectation. vsim is too slow to swallow the full unit-test harness as-is. Before running a Kanata loop-window dump, the human/LLM driving `/optimize` must shrink everything between `_start` and the kernel call:

- Drop the input `test_Fread` (preload a small in-memory buffer, or a static array).
- Drop the golden `test_Fread`.
- Drop `verify_buffer` and any post-kernel accuracy check.
- Optionally cut the input size (e.g., `size = 65535` -> something just large enough that the inner loop reaches steady state, plus the warmup `--kanata-loop-skip` count).

Two ways to do this in practice:

- Edit `t_<fn>.c` directly into a stripped variant for vsim runs.
- Use `hot-inner-kernel-extractor` (already in `.claude/skills/`) which builds a synthetic harness with the kernel input preloaded statically - no fread, no golden, no verify.

---

## Why this matters for `/optimize`

The clean-looking idea was:

```
hotloops -> (start_pc, end_pc) -> vsim Kanata gate on full perf ELF -> pipeline log
```

That doesn't work as written: the gate bugs (Issues 1 and 2) and the harness-prelude wall (the fread/verify scaffolding around the kernel) make it unusable.

The working plan:

```
hotloops on full perf ELF (works)
  |
  v  start_pc, end_pc, picked by human/LLM from the symbol-named menu
  |
slim the harness:
  - hot-inner-kernel-extractor (synthetic harness, kernel input static), OR
  - edit t_<fn>.c to drop fread / golden / verify_buffer
  |
  v  small ELF that hits the kernel in milliseconds
  |
vsim Kanata gate on the slim ELF (still need Issues 1 and 2 fixed)
  |
  v Konata pipeline trace
```

---

## What needs to be filed before integration

Against `hotloops.sh`: add a baremetal/system-mode path so it can launch `qemu-system-riscv64 -M ... -cpu ... -bios <elf>` instead of user-mode `qemu-riscv64 -- <elf>`.

Against vsim: the loop-window gate doesn't gate (Issue 1) and doesn't exit on `record` complete (Issue 2). Reproducer:

- ELF: `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/work/t_relu_s8_perf.adx`
- Flags: `--kanata-loop-start 0x105f4 --kanata-loop-end 0x10642 --kanata-loop-skip 0 --kanata-loop-record 4`

Both need fixing before vsim Kanata can be a useful direct target.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Hot inner-loop detector wrapper (system-mode, baremetal RISC-V).
#
# Runs a baremetal ELF under qemu-system-<arch> with the hotloops plugin
# and prints a sorted table of detected backward branches:
#
#   iters | start_pc -> end_pc | function:offset
#
# Usage:
#   scripts/hotloops.sh [opts] <elf>
#
# Options:
#   --arch <riscv32|riscv64|auto>   Target arch (default: auto)
#   --qemu <path>                   Override qemu-system binary
#   --plugin <path>                 Override plugin .so
#   --machine <name>                -M <name> (default andes_ae350)
#   --cpu <spec>                    -cpu <spec>
#                                   (default andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024)
#   --qemu-config <file>            -readconfig <file> (e.g. AE350 device cfg)
#   --cwd <dir>                     chdir before launching QEMU so semihosting
#                                   opens (fread of bin/*.bin) resolve
#   --qemu-arg <arg>                Append an extra arg to the QEMU command
#                                   (repeatable)
#   --limit <N>                     Top-N loops to print (default 50)
#   --min-iters <N>                 Drop loops with fewer than N iters (default 1)
#   --objdump <path>                Override objdump (resolves symbols)
#   --addr2line <path>              Override addr2line (resolves source lines)
#   --no-source                     Disable source file:line resolution
#   --raw                           Print raw plugin output, no formatting
#   -h | --help                     Show help

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd -- "$(dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
QEMU_ROOT="$(cd -- "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

ARCH="auto"
QEMU=""
PLUGIN=""
LIMIT=50
MIN_ITERS=1
OBJDUMP=""
ADDR2LINE=""
NO_SOURCE=0
RAW=0
MACHINE="andes_ae350"
CPU="andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
QEMU_CONFIG=""
QEMU_CWD=""
EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS=()

usage() { sed -n '2,30p' "$0"; exit 0; }

# --- arg parsing ---
ELF=""
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --arch)        ARCH="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu)        QEMU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --plugin)      PLUGIN="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --machine)     MACHINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --cpu)         CPU="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu-config) QEMU_CONFIG="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --cwd)         QEMU_CWD="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --qemu-arg)    EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS+=("$2"); shift 2 ;;
        --limit)       LIMIT="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --min-iters)   MIN_ITERS="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --objdump)     OBJDUMP="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --addr2line)   ADDR2LINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --no-source)   NO_SOURCE=1; shift ;;
        --raw)         RAW=1; shift ;;
        -h|--help)     usage ;;
        -*) echo "unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 2 ;;
        *)
            if [[ -z "$ELF" ]]; then
                ELF="$1"; shift
            else
                echo "error: unexpected positional arg: $1" >&2; exit 2
            fi ;;
    esac
done

[[ -n "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: ELF path required" >&2; exit 2; }
[[ -f "$ELF" ]] || { echo "error: not found: $ELF" >&2; exit 2; }

# --- arch detection ---
if [[ "$ARCH" == "auto" ]]; then
    file_out="$(file -L -- "$ELF" 2>/dev/null || true)"
    case "$file_out" in
        *"ELF 64-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv64 ;;
        *"ELF 32-bit"*"RISC-V"*)  ARCH=riscv32 ;;
        *) echo "error: cannot auto-detect arch (not RISC-V?), pass --arch" >&2; exit 2 ;;
    esac
fi

# --- locate qemu + plugin ---
if [[ -z "$QEMU" ]]; then
    QEMU="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/qemu-system-${ARCH}"
fi
[[ -x "$QEMU" ]] || { echo "error: qemu not found/executable: $QEMU" >&2; exit 2; }

if [[ -z "$PLUGIN" ]]; then
    PLUGIN="${QEMU_ROOT}/build/contrib/plugins/libhotloops.so"
fi
[[ -f "$PLUGIN" ]] || { echo "error: plugin not built: $PLUGIN" >&2; exit 2; }

# --- run ---
LOG="$(mktemp -t hotloops.XXXXXX.log)"
trap 'rm -f "$LOG"' EXIT

QEMU_CMD=("$QEMU"
          -plugin "${PLUGIN},limit=${LIMIT},min_iters=${MIN_ITERS}"
          -d plugin -D "$LOG"
          -M "$MACHINE"
          -cpu "$CPU"
          -nographic -semihosting
          -bios "$ELF")
[[ -n "$QEMU_CONFIG" ]] && QEMU_CMD+=(-readconfig "$QEMU_CONFIG")
[[ ${#EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]} -gt 0 ]] && QEMU_CMD+=("${EXTRA_QEMU_ARGS[@]}")
if [[ -n "$QEMU_CWD" ]]; then
    ( cd "$QEMU_CWD" && "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" ) >/dev/null
else
    "${QEMU_CMD[@]}" >/dev/null
fi

if [[ "$RAW" -eq 1 ]]; then
    cat "$LOG"
    exit 0
fi

# --- symbol table from objdump (best effort) ---
if [[ -z "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
    for cand in llvm-objdump riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump \
                riscv32-unknown-elf-objdump objdump; do
        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            OBJDUMP="$cand"
            break
        fi
    done
fi

SYMS="$(mktemp -t hotloops_syms.XXXXXX)"
LINES="$(mktemp -t hotloops_lines.XXXXXX)"
trap 'rm -f "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES"' EXIT
if [[ -n "$OBJDUMP" ]]; then
    # Capture FUNC symbols: addr size type name
    "$OBJDUMP" -t "$ELF" 2>/dev/null \
        | awk 'tolower($0) ~ /[ \t]f[ \t]/ && NF>=5 {
                  addr=strtonum("0x" $1);
                  size=strtonum("0x" $(NF-1));
                  name=$NF;
                  if (size>0) print addr, size, name;
              }' \
        | sort -n -k1,1 > "$SYMS" || true
fi

# --- locate addr2line for source line resolution (DWARF) ---
if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -z "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
    for cand in llvm-addr2line addr2line \
                riscv64-unknown-elf-addr2line riscv32-unknown-elf-addr2line \
                riscv64-linux-gnu-addr2line; do
        if command -v "$cand" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            ADDR2LINE="$cand"
            break
        fi
    done
fi

# Pre-collect all PCs from the log and batch-resolve to file:line.
# LINES file format: <hex-pc> <file:line>
if [[ "$NO_SOURCE" -eq 0 && -n "$ADDR2LINE" ]]; then
    pcs="$(awk -F',' '/^0x/ {gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$1); gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$2); print $1; print $2}' "$LOG" | sort -u)"
    if [[ -n "$pcs" ]]; then
        # addr2line: -e <elf> -f -s -C reads addrs on stdin, prints
        # alternating function/file:line; we ask for file:line only via -p.
        out="$(printf '%s\n' $pcs | "$ADDR2LINE" -e "$ELF" -s 2>/dev/null || true)"
        paste <(printf '%s\n' $pcs) <(printf '%s\n' "$out") > "$LINES" || true
    fi
fi

# --- format ---
python3 - "$LOG" "$SYMS" "$LINES" "$LIMIT" "$MIN_ITERS" <<'PY'
import sys, re

log_path = sys.argv[1]
syms_path = sys.argv[2]
lines_path = sys.argv[3]
limit = int(sys.argv[4])
min_iters = int(sys.argv[5])

syms = []
try:
    with open(syms_path) as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.split()
            if len(parts) >= 3:
                addr = int(parts[0])
                size = int(parts[1])
                name = " ".join(parts[2:])
                syms.append((addr, size, name))
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

def resolve(pc):
    for addr, size, name in syms:
        if addr <= pc < addr + size:
            return f"{name}+0x{pc-addr:x}"
    return "?"

src_map = {}
try:
    with open(lines_path) as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.rstrip("\n").split("\t", 1)
            if len(parts) == 2:
                pc_str, src = parts
                src = src.strip()
                if src and not src.startswith("??"):
                    try:
                        src_map[int(pc_str, 16)] = src
                    except ValueError:
                        pass
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

def src_line(pc):
    return src_map.get(pc, "")

rows = []
with open(log_path) as f:
    for line in f:
        m = re.match(r"\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+)\s*,\s*(\d+)", line)
        if m:
            rows.append((int(m.group(1), 16),
                         int(m.group(2), 16),
                         int(m.group(3))))

if not rows:
    sys.stderr.write("hotloops: no backward edges observed\n")
    sys.exit(0)

rows = [r for r in rows if r[2] >= min_iters]
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r[2])
rows = rows[:limit]

if not rows:
    sys.stderr.write(f"hotloops: no loops with iters >= {min_iters}\n")
    sys.exit(0)

print(f"# hot loops (iters >= {min_iters}), sorted desc, top {len(rows)}")

have_src = bool(src_map)

w_iter = max(len("iters"), max(len(str(r[2])) for r in rows))
w_pc = 18  # 0x + 16 hex
w_sym = max(len("location"),
            max(len(f"{resolve(r[0])} -> {resolve(r[1])}") for r in rows))

if have_src:
    def srcpair(s, e):
        a, b = src_line(s), src_line(e)
        if a and b and a == b:
            return a
        return f"{a or '?'} -> {b or '?'}"
    w_src = max(len("source"),
                max(len(srcpair(r[0], r[1])) for r in rows))
    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | "
          f"{'location':<{w_sym}} | source")
    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym + 3 + w_src))
    for s, e, c in rows:
        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | "
              f"{loc:<{w_sym}} | {srcpair(s, e)}")
else:
    print(f"{'iters':>{w_iter}} | {'start_pc':<{w_pc}} -> {'end_pc':<{w_pc}} | location")
    print("-" * (w_iter + 3 + w_pc + 4 + w_pc + 3 + w_sym))
    for s, e, c in rows:
        loc = f"{resolve(s)} -> {resolve(e)}"
        print(f"{c:>{w_iter}} | 0x{s:016x} -> 0x{e:016x} | {loc}")

# Kanata Loop-Window Pipeline Dump

Restrict a Kanata or Clarity pipeline dump to a chosen number of iterations of one specific loop body, after a configurable warmup. The simulator exits as soon as the window closes and reports per-window IPC.

## When to use it

- Inspect steady-state pipeline behavior of a hot loop in Konata.
- Get IPC for the loop body alone, ignoring warmup and tail effects.
- Produce a small, shareable pipeline trace instead of a multi-GB full-run log.

## Flags

All flags are optional and only take effect with `--kanata` or `--clarity`. `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must be specified together.

| Flag | Argument | Default | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `--kanata-loop-start`  | `<pc>` | -  | Start PC of the loop body. Reported in the summary; not used to gate iterations. Hex (`0x...`) or decimal accepted. |
| `--kanata-loop-end`    | `<pc>` | -  | End PC of the loop body. Each retire of this PC counts as one iteration. |
| `--kanata-loop-skip`   | `<N>`  | 0  | Warmup iterations to ignore before the window opens. `0` records from the first iteration. |
| `--kanata-loop-record` | `<X>`  | 1  | Iterations to capture after warmup. Must be >= 1. Simulator exits when this many iterations have retired. |

## How iterations are counted

An iteration is counted only when the instruction at `--kanata-loop-end` __retires__. Mispredicted or flushed fetches of that PC do not advance the counter, so the recorded window is always exactly `record` complete iterations of the loop body.

## Picking the start/end PCs

Use `objdump -d` on your ELF and pick:

- `start` = first instruction of the loop body (typically the branch target / loop label).
- `end`   = last instruction of the loop body (typically the back-branch, or the instruction immediately before it if you only want taken iterations).

Both PCs must be reachable in the simulated execution. If `end` is never retired, the window never opens and the simulator runs to completion normally.

## Output

While the gate is closed (warmup), no `I` / `C` / `R` records are emitted, but the internal cycle counter still advances so timing inside the window is accurate. When the window closes, the logger writes to spdlog:

```
Kanata loop window [pc 0x...start..0x...end]: <iters> iters, <insns> insns, <cycles> cycles, IPC=<ipc>
```

and the simulator exits with status 0. The dump file contains a self-consistent Kanata trace for those iterations only and can be opened directly in Konata.

## Example

```
vsim --elf bench.elf \
     --kanata bench.kanata \
     --kanata-loop-start 0x80001234 \
     --kanata-loop-end   0x80001260 \
     --kanata-loop-skip  100 \
     --kanata-loop-record 8
```

Skips the first 100 iterations, records the next 8, prints the IPC summary, and exits. `bench.kanata` contains those 8 iterations only.

## Constraints

- `--kanata-loop-*` requires `--kanata` or `--clarity`; otherwise startup fails.
- `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must both be present or both absent.
- `--kanata-loop-record` must be >= 1.
- `--kanata` and `--clarity` remain mutually exclusive.

# HARD GATE - read before any edit
Before calling Edit, Write, or NotebookEdit on a non-test file, you MUST:
1. Identify the smallest behavior the change adds or fixes.
2. Have an existing failing test for that behavior visible in this session,
   either by writing one in this turn OR by running the suite and showing
   its failing output. The test must reference the behavior, not just compile.
3. State which TDD phase the next edit belongs to: RED (writing the test),
   GREEN (smallest code to pass), or REFACTOR (tests already green).

If steps 1-2 are not satisfied, STOP. Do not edit the non-test file.
Instead, write the failing test first: pick the test file, draft the
smallest test that captures the behavior, run it, and confirm it fails
for the right reason. Only then proceed to the implementation edit.

For commits: the message MUST start with "structural:" or "behavioral:".
Mixed-purpose commits are forbidden - split them.

This gate applies even when the user asks to "just implement X". The
correct response is to write the test first, then implement - not to
skip the test because the user did not mention it.

# ROLE AND EXPERTISE

You are a senior software engineer who follows Test-Driven Development (TDD) and Tidy First principles. Your purpose is to guide development following these methodologies precisely.

# CORE DEVELOPMENT PRINCIPLES

- Always follow the TDD cycle: Red → Green → Refactor Refactor Refacot
- Write the simplest failing test first
- Implement the minimum code needed to make tests pass
- Refactor only after tests are passing
- Follow "Tidy First" approach by separating structural changes from behavioral changes
- Maintain high code quality throughout development, always apply the modern design patterns.
* Never workaround issues! find the real root causes and apply the proper solution. No quick fix. The slowest but proper solution is the fastest solution. is the fastest solution.
* If the code snippet needs to be changed, prefer removing all of them and rewrite completely. Don't patch the legacy code. Remove them and rewrite . Don't patch the legacy code. Remove them and rewrite. 

# TDD METHODOLOGY GUIDANCE

- Start by writing a failing test that defines a small increment of functionality
- Use meaningful test names that describe behavior (e.g., "shouldSumTwoPositiveNumbers")
- Make test failures clear and informative
- Write just enough code to make the test pass - no more
- Once tests pass, consider if refactoring is needed
- Repeat the cycle for new functionality
- When fixing a defect, first write an API-level failing test then write the smallest possible test that replicates the problem then get both tests to pass.

# TIDY FIRST APPROACH

- Separate all changes into two distinct types:
  1. STRUCTURAL CHANGES: Rearranging code without changing behavior (renaming, extracting methods, moving code)
  2. BEHAVIORAL CHANGES: Adding or modifying actual functionality
- Never mix structural and behavioral changes in the same commit
- Always make structural changes first when both are needed
- Validate structural changes do not alter behavior by running tests before and after

# COMMIT DISCIPLINE

- Only commit when:
  1. ALL tests are passing
  2. ALL compiler/linter warnings have been resolved
  3. The change represents a single logical unit of work
  4. Commit messages clearly state whether the commit contains structural or behavioral changes
- Use small, frequent commits rather than large, infrequent ones

# CODE QUALITY STANDARDS

- Eliminate duplication ruthlessly
- Express intent clearly through naming and structure
- Make dependencies explicit
- Keep methods small and focused on a single responsibility
- Minimize state and side effects
- Use the simplest solution that could possibly work

# REFACTORING GUIDELINES

- Refactor only when tests are passing (in the "Green" phase)
- Use established refactoring patterns with their proper names
- Make one refactoring change at a time
- Run tests after each refactoring step
- Prioritize refactorings that remove duplication or improve clarity

# EXAMPLE WORKFLOW

When approaching a new feature:

1. Write a simple failing test for a small part of the feature
2. Implement the bare minimum to make it pass
3. Run tests to confirm they pass (Green)
4. Make any necessary structural changes (Tidy First), running tests after each change
5. Commit structural changes separately
6. Add another test for the next small increment of functionality
7. Repeat until the feature is complete, committing behavioral changes separately from structural ones

Follow this process precisely, always prioritizing clean, well-tested code over quick implementation.

Always write one test at a time, make it run, then improve structure. Always run all the tests (except long-running tests) each time.
